title: Cestode Infections
authors: A. Clinton White, Jr., Miguel M. Cabada

items.title: Understanding Human Roles in Cestode Life Cycle
items.content: Humans can serve as definitive or intermediate hosts for various cestodes, impacting the clinical presentation and management of infections. For tapeworms like Taenia saginata, humans are definitive hosts with adult worms residing in the gastrointestinal tract. Conversely, for Echinococcus spp., Spirometra spp., and Taenia multiceps, humans act as intermediate or dead-end hosts where larval-stage parasites inhabit tissues.
keyPoints: Humans are definitive hosts for T. saginata, D. latus, and D. caninum with adult tapeworms in the gastrointestinal tract.
Infections like Echinococcus spp., Spirometra spp., Taenia multiceps involve humans as intermediate or dead-end hosts with larval-stage parasites in tissues.
Taenia solium involves humans as both intermediate and definitive hosts.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Cestode Infections

items.title: Life Cycle of Tapeworms: Key Stages and Host Interactions
items.content: The life cycle of tapeworms involves multiple stages, requiring at least one intermediate host for most human infections. After ingestion by an intermediate host, eggs or proglottids release invasive larvae (oncospheres) that penetrate the intestinal mucosa and develop into encysted forms such as cysticercus, coenurus, or hydatid. The definitive host ingests these cysts, leading to scolex evagination and tapeword development.
keyPoints: Tapeworm life cycle involves ingestion of eggs/proglottids by an intermediate host.
Oncospheres penetrate the intestinal mucosa and develop into encysted forms (cysticercus, coenurus, hydatid).
Definitive host ingests cysts, leading to scolex evagination and tapeworm development.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Cestode Infections

items.title: Diagnosis and Detection Methods for Taeniasis
items.content: The diagnosis of taeniasis saginata and taeniasis asiatica relies on the detection of eggs or proglottids in stool samples. Eggs may not always be present in stool but can sometimes be found in the perianal region, necessitating examination with a cellophane-tape swab. Antigen-detection assays offer higher sensitivity than microscopic examination, though they are not commercially available.
keyPoints: Diagnosis depends on detection of eggs or proglottids in stool.
Eggs may be present in the perianal area; use cellophane-tape swab for examination.
Antigen-detection assays are more sensitive than microscopic examination but not commercially available.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SAGINATA AND TAENIASIS ASIATICA

items.title: Morphological Characteristics of Taenia Species
items.content: T. saginata and T. asiatica have similar clinical manifestations but can be distinguished morphologically by their scolex structures: T. asiatica has an unarmed rostellum with four suckers, while T. saginata lacks a rostellum and attaches via four prominent suckers. Each gravid segment of these species contains 15–30 uterine branches, compared to 8–12 in T. solium.
keyPoints: T. asiatica has an unarmed rostellum with four suckers; T. saginata lacks a rostellum and uses four prominent suckers for attachment.
Each gravid segment of T. saginata and T. asiatica contains 15–30 uterine branches, unlike the 8–12 in T. solium.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SAGINATA AND TAENIASIS ASIATICA

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Effective Treatment of Taeniasis
items.content: A single dose of praziquantel (10 mg/kg) is highly effective for treating taeniasis caused by T. saginata and T. asiatica.
keyPoints: Praziquantel dosage: 10 mg/kg in a single dose
Niclosamide as an alternative, less available
Nitazoxanide can also be used
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Prevention of Cysticercosis
items.content: Adequate cooking or specific temperature exposure can prevent cysticercosis by destroying the parasites in beef.
keyPoints: Cooking temperatures: $56^{	ext{o}}$C for 5 min or below
Refrigeration, salting, freezing at $-10^{	ext{o}}$C for 9 days also effective
General measures include inspection of viscera and proper feces disposal
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Diagnosis of Neurocysticercosis
items.content: Neurocysticercosis diagnosis relies on neuroimaging to identify cystic lesions containing a scolex, supplemented by clinical presentation and other tests.
keyPoints: Definitive diagnosis with histologic observation or fundusscopic visualization
High-resolution neuroimaging can show characteristic scolex
Clinical diagnosis may use antigen-detection, PCR, or next-generation sequencing in CSF
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Neurocysticercosis via Neuroimaging
items.content: Neuroimaging is essential in diagnosing neurocysticercosis, with the presence of a cystic lesion containing a scolex being diagnostic. Key findings include cystic lesions with or without enhancement, nodular calcifications, focal enhancing lesions, and cysts in subarachnoid spaces.
keyPoints: Cystic lesions with a 'dot-in-hole' appearance (scolex) are diagnostic.
Cysticerci in brain parenchyma usually measure 5–20 mm in diameter.
Subarachnoid cystic lesions can enlarge up to 6 cm and may be lobulated.
MRI is more sensitive than CT for small cystic lesions, scolexes, and enhancement.
CT is more sensitive for identifying calcified lesions.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria

items.subtopic: TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria
items.title: Interpretation of Neurocysticercosis Findings with Exposure History
items.content: The interpretation of neuroimaging findings in suspected neurocysticercosis is significantly influenced by the patient's exposure history to T. solium, alongside specific antibody or antigen detection.
keyPoints: Exposure history modifies the interpretation of neuroimaging studies for neurocysticercosis.
Detection of antibodies or antigens of T. solium are major criteria supporting diagnosis.
Histologic demonstration of the parasite from a brain or spinal cord lesion is an absolute criterion.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria

items.subtopic: b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria
items.title: Neurocysticercosis Imaging and Serological Confirmation
items.content: Confirmatory diagnosis of neurocysticercosis can be achieved through specific imaging findings and serological tests. Resolution of cystic lesions after therapy, migration of ventricular cysts on sequential studies, and detection of anticysticercal antibodies or antigens are crucial.
keyPoints: Resolution of cystic lesions spontaneously or post-cysticidal drug therapy
Migration of ventricular cysts documented on sequential neuroimaging
Detection of specific anticysticercal antibodies (e.g., EITB) with >99% specificity and sensitivity in patients with multiple cysts
Serum samples are more sensitive than CSF using EITB
Antigen-detection assays commercially available in Europe but not the U.S.
Real-time PCR employed for diagnosis of extraparenchymal disease
items.reference: Cestode Infections · b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria

items.subtopic: b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria
items.title: Clinical and Exposure Criteria for Neurocysticercosis Diagnosis
items.content: Diagnosis of neurocysticercosis can be supported by major exposure criteria, such as detection of cysticercosis outside the CNS or evidence of household contact with T. solium infection, alongside clinical manifestations.
keyPoints: Cysticercosis outside the central nervous system (e.g., cigar-shaped calcifications in muscle)
Evidence of a household contact with T. solium infection
Clinical symptoms suggestive of neurocysticercosis include seizures or obstructive hydrocephalus
Presence on CT of round lesions 5–20 mm in diameter with no midline shift almost always indicates neurocysticercosis in endemic areas
items.reference: Cestode Infections · b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria

items.subtopic: Taenia solium and Cysticercosis | INITIAL MANAGEMENT OF NEUROCYSTICERCOSIS
items.title: Seizure Management in Neurocysticercosis
items.content: In neurocysticercosis, initial management prioritizes seizure control using antiseizure medications. These can often be tapered after 6 months if imaging normalizes and no breakthrough seizures occur for single enhancing lesions. For multiple parenchymal lesions, therapy is prolonged but may taper off after 2 years if lesions resolve without calcification and patients remain seizure-free.
keyPoints: Seizure medications can usually be tapered after 6 months in patients with single enhancing lesions if imaging normalizes and no breakthrough seizures occur.
Patients with multiple parenchymal lesions require more prolonged therapy, potentially tapering off after 2 years if lesions resolve without calcification and patients remain seizure-free.
Calcified lesions increase the risk of recurrent seizures, especially if associated with perilesional edema or enhancement.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taenia solium and Cysticercosis | INITIAL MANAGEMENT OF NEUROCYSTICERCOSIS | MANAGING HYDROCEPHALUS | ANTIPARASITIC DRUGS AND ANTI-INFLAMMATORY THERAPY

items.subtopic: Taenia solium and Cysticercosis | MANAGING HYDROCEPHALUS
items.title: Management of Hydrocephalus in Neurocysticercosis
items.content: In neurocysticercosis, managing hydrocephalus involves reducing intracranial pressure. Surgical removal of cysticerci from cerebral ventricles is preferred to alleviate obstructive hydrocephalus. Antiparasitic drugs should be avoided before surgery as they make cysticerci more friable. If complete removal isn't possible, shunting procedures like ventriculoperitoneal shunting are used, with antiparasitics and glucocorticoids potentially reducing shunt failure risk.
keyPoints: Reduction of intracranial pressure is a priority in managing hydrocephalus due to neurocysticercosis.
Surgical removal of cysticerci from cerebral ventricles is preferred for obstructive hydrocephalus.
Antiparasitic drugs should be avoided before surgery as they make cysticerci more friable.
Shunt failure risk may be reduced by administering antiparasitic drugs and glucocorticoids post-surgery.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taenia solium and Cysticercosis | INITIAL MANAGEMENT OF NEUROCYSTICERCOSIS | MANAGING HYDROCEPHALUS | ANTIPARASITIC DRUGS AND ANTI-INFLAMMATORY THERAPY

items.subtopic: Taenia solium and Cysticercosis | ANTIPARASITIC DRUGS AND ANTI-INFLAMMATORY THERAPY
items.title: Antiparasitic Drug Use in Neurocysticercosis
items.content: Antiparasitic drugs are not an emergency treatment for neurocysticercosis and should be delayed until patients are stabilized with antiseizure and anti-inflammatory medications. They should not be started if intracranial pressure is elevated. Albendazole (15 mg/kg per day for 8–28 days) or a combination of albendazole and praziquantel (50 mg/kg per day) is recommended, especially in patients with multiple cystic lesions. High-dose glucocorticoids are used to mitigate inflammatory responses during treatment.
keyPoints: Antiparasitic drugs should not be started until patients are stabilized with antiseizure and anti-inflammatory medications.
Intracranial pressure must be normal before starting antiparasitic therapy.
Albendazole (15 mg/kg per day for 8–28 days) is recommended, especially in viable parenchymal cysticercosis.
Combination of albendazole and praziquantel (50 mg/kg per day) is more effective for patients with multiple cystic lesions.
High-dose glucocorticoids are used to mitigate inflammatory responses during antiparasitic treatment.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taenia solium and Cysticercosis | INITIAL MANAGEMENT OF NEUROCYSTICERCOSIS | MANAGING HYDROCEPHALUS | ANTIPARASITIC DRUGS AND ANTI-INFLAMMATORY THERAPY

items.title: Etiology and Life Cycle of Echinococcus Species
items.content: Echinococcosis is caused by the larval stages of Echinococcus species, primarily E. granulosus sensu lato and E. multilocularis. The life cycle involves both intermediate hosts (e.g., sheep, cattle for E. granulosus; rodents for E. multilocularis) and definitive hosts (usually canines). Humans are incidental dead-end hosts.
keyPoints: Echinococcus species include E. granulosus sensu lato, E. multilocularis, and E. vogeli.
Definitive hosts: dogs, foxes, wolves; intermediate hosts: sheep, cattle, rodents.
Humans are dead-end hosts, not part of the life cycle.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology and Cyst Development in Echinococcus Infections
items.content: E. granulosus sensu lato forms fluid-filled unilocular hydatid cysts, while E. multilocularis causes multilocular, invasive lesions. The cysts develop slowly over years, potentially containing thousands of protoscolices.
keyPoints: E. granulosus develops into unilocular hydatid cysts with daughter cysts and brood capsules.
E. multilocularis forms multilocular, invasive lesions without brood capsules or protoscolices.
Cysts expand slowly over years.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Organ Involvement in Echinococcosis
items.content: Echinococcal cysts are often asymptomatic until they grow large enough to cause organ-specific symptoms. The liver is most commonly affected, followed by the lungs. Cysts may rupture, causing allergic reactions.
keyPoints: Cysts remain asymptomatic for years; liver involved in two-thirds of E. granulosus and nearly all E. multilocularis cases.
Lungs are involved in about 20% of E. granulosus cases.
Rupture can lead to type I allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Symptoms and Complications of Hepatic Cystic Echinococcosis
items.content: Hepatic cystic echinococcosis can present with abdominal fullness, pain, or a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant. It may mimic cholelithiasis due to bile duct compression or cyst fluid leakage into the biliary tree. Severe complications include rupture leading to fever, pruritus, urticaria, eosinophilia, or anaphylaxis.
keyPoints: Symptoms: abdominal fullness, pain, palpable mass in right upper quadrant
Complications: bile duct compression, cyst fluid leakage, jaundice, rupture with systemic symptoms (fever, pruritus, urticaria, eosinophilia, anaphylaxis)
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Imaging of Echinococcal Cysts
items.content: Imaging studies are crucial for diagnosing echinococcal cysts. Ultrasound, CT, or MRI can identify lesions in solid organs like the liver. WHO classification aids management decisions. Specific imaging features include well-defined cyst walls, internal trabeculation, calcifications, and hydatid sand.
keyPoints: Imaging modalities: ultrasound, CT, MRI
Diagnostic features: well-defined cyst walls, internal trabeculation, calcifications, hydatid sand
WHO classification for management
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Serodiagnostic Assays and Their Limitations in Echinococcosis
items.content: Serodiagnostic assays, such as ELISA and immunoblot, are useful but have limitations. They show ~90% sensitivity for hydatid liver disease but only ~50% for pulmonary cysts. Aspiration of cyst fluids can confirm diagnosis by identifying photoscolices or hooklets, though it carries risks.
keyPoints: Serodiagnostic assays: ELISA and immunoblot
Sensitivity: ~90% for hydatid liver disease, ~50% for pulmonary cysts
Aspiration risks: potential fluid leakage, dissemination of infection, anaphylactic reactions
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Management of Cystic Echinococcosis Based on Size, Stage, and Location
items.content: Treatment strategies for cystic echinococcosis vary according to the size, stage, location, and clinical manifestations of the cysts. Smaller lesions (CL, CE1, and CE3a) less than 5 cm in diameter may be effectively treated with albendazole alone. Larger or more complex liver cysts can often be managed using PAIR, which involves percutaneous aspiration, instillation of scolicidal agents, and reaspiration.
keyPoints: Smaller cysts (<5 cm) such as CL, CE1, and CE3a respond well to albendazole alone.
PAIR is suitable for larger CE1 lesions and uncomplicated CE3a lesions in the liver.
Contraindications for PAIR include cysts communicating with the biliary tree, large cysts (>10 cm), or superficial cysts.
Albendazole (15 mg/kg daily) should be started 2 days before PAIR and continued for at least 4 weeks post-procedure.
PAIR can achieve cure rates equivalent to surgery with less morbidity.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · CYSTIC HYDATID DISEASE

items.title: Surgical Intervention in Complicated Cystic Echinococcosis Cases
items.content: Surgery is the preferred treatment for complicated cystic echinococcosis, such as when cysts communicate with the biliary tract or are located in thoracic and intracranial regions. Pericystectomy is recommended to remove liver cysts completely along with surrounding fibrous tissue to prevent spillage and recurrence.
keyPoints: Surgery is indicated for complicated cases, including those involving communication with the biliary tract.
Pericystectomy involves removing the entire cyst and surrounding fibrous tissue.
Laparoscopic or robotic surgery can be safely performed by experienced practitioners.
Albendazole (10-15 mg/kg) should be administered before and after surgical resection for several weeks.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · CYSTIC HYDATID DISEASE

items.title: Management and Treatment of Alveolar Hydatid Disease
items.content: Surgical resection is the primary treatment for E. multilocularis infection, aiming for complete removal to cure the disease. Post-surgery, albendazole therapy should continue for at least 2 years. In cases where complete resection isn't possible, indefinite albendazole treatment with careful monitoring is necessary.
keyPoints: Surgical resection offers the best chance for cure of E. multilocularis infection.
Albendazole treatment post-surgery should last at least 2 years if presumed curative.
In cases where complete resection isn't possible, albendazole is continued indefinitely with monitoring.
Positron emission tomography can be used to monitor disease activity.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ALVEOLAR HYDATID DISEASE | RODENTOLEPIS NANA (PREVIOUSLY HYMENOLEPIS NANA)

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Features of Rodentolepis nana Infection
items.content: Rodentolepis nana, the most common human cestode infection, is diagnosed by identifying its eggs in stool microscopy. The infection often remains asymptomatic but can cause diarrhea, abdominal pain, and weight loss, especially in children.
keyPoints: R. nana is the most common human cestode infection.
Diagnosis involves finding characteristic R. nana eggs (30–44 µm) in stool microscopy.
Infection is often asymptomatic but may cause diarrhea, abdominal pain, and weight loss.
Eggs are immediately infective after leaving the host and survive for ≤10 days.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ALVEOLAR HYDATID DISEASE | RODENTOLEPIS NANA (PREVIOUSLY HYMENOLEPIS NANA)

items.title: Treatment and Prevention of Rodentolepis nana Infection
items.content: Praziquantel is the treatment of choice for R. nana, effective against both adult worms and cystic-cercoids in the intestinal wall. Nitazoxanide serves as an alternative. Preventive measures include improved sanitation and personal hygiene to interrupt the fecal-oral transmission route.
keyPoints: Praziquantel dosage: 25 mg/kg once
Alternative treatment: Nitazoxanide (500 mg twice a day for 3 days)
Prevention focuses on sanitation, hand washing, and hygiene to control epidemics
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Rodentolepis nana Infection | HYMENOLEPIASIS DIMINUTA | DIPHYLLOBOTHRIASIS (DIBOTHRIOCEPHALUS/ADENOCEPHALUS)

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hymenolepis diminuta Infection
items.content: H. diminuta infection is diagnosed by detecting eggs in the stool, acquired through ingestion of contaminated food. Praziquantel (25 mg/kg once) is the treatment of choice.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: Detection of eggs in stool
Transmission: Ingesting uncooked cereal and other foods contaminated with fleas or insects
Treatment: Praziquantel (25 mg/kg once)
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Rodentolepis nana Infection | HYMENOLEPIASIS DIMINUTA | DIPHYLLOBOTHRIASIS (DIBOTHRIOCEPHALUS/ADENOCEPHALUS)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Diphyllobothriasis
items.content: Most infections are asymptomatic, but some patients may experience abdominal discomfort, diarrhea, vomiting, weakness, or weight loss. Severe cases can lead to vitamin B12 deficiency due to the tapeworm's interference with absorption. Diagnosis involves detecting characteristic eggs in stool and examining proglottids.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic infections are common
Symptoms: Abdominal discomfort, diarrhea, vomiting, weakness, weight loss
Vitamin B12 deficiency risk due to tapeworm's avid receptors
Diagnosis: Detection of eggs in stool with a single shell and operculum/knob; examination of proglottids
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Rodentolepis nana Infection | HYMENOLEPIASIS DIMINUTA | DIPHYLLOBOTHRIASIS (DIBOTHRIOCEPHALUS/ADENOCEPHALUS)

items.title: Treatment and Prevention of Diphyllobothriasis
items.content: Praziquantel is the treatment of choice for diphyllobothriasis, effective against all Diphyllobothriid species. In cases of vitamin B12 deficiency due to infection, parenteral vitamin B12 may be administered.
keyPoints: Praziquantel dosage: 5–10 mg/kg once
Prevention methods include heating fish to 54°C for 5 min or freezing at -18°C for 24 h. Brining with high salt concentration can also kill larvae.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Diphyllobothriasis | DIPYLIDIASIS | SPARGANOSIS | COENUROSIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Dipylidiasis
items.content: Dipylidium caninum infections are diagnosed by detecting motile proglottids or characteristic egg packets in stool. Praziquantel is the treatment of choice, with prevention focusing on antiparasitic and flea treatments for pets.
keyPoints: Children more often infected than adults
Symptoms include passage of motile proglottids resembling flattened grains of rice
Praziquantel is the treatment of choice
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Diphyllobothriasis | DIPYLIDIASIS | SPARGANOSIS | COENUROSIS

items.question: Which cestodes have humans as both intermediate and definitive hosts?
items.explanation: Humans can be both intermediate and definitive hosts for Taenia solium.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Cestode Infections

items.question: What is the term used to describe the elongating chain of attached proglottids in tapeworms?
items.explanation: The strobila is the elongating chain of attached proglottids in tapeworms.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Cestode Infections

items.question: Which form do oncospheres develop into after penetrating tissues?
items.explanation: Oncospheres develop into a cysticercus, coenurus, or hydatid after penetrating tissues.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Cestode Infections

items.question: Which intermediate host is associated with Taenia asiatica?
items.explanation: Taenia asiatica uses pigs as its intermediate host.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SAGINATA AND TAENIASIS ASIATICA

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of the scolex of Taenia saginata compared to Taenia asiatica?
options: T. saginata has hooks on its rostellum
T. saginata has an unarmed rostellum and four suckers
T. saginata has more uterine branches
items.explanation: Taenia saginata has no rostellum but attaches via four prominent suckers, unlike T. asiatica which has an unarmed rostellum.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SAGINATA AND TAENIASIS ASIATICA

items.question: How are the eggs of Taenia saginata and Taenia asiatica differentiated from those of Taenia solium?
options: By number of uterine branches in gravid segments
By presence of hooks on the rostellum
items.explanation: The eggs are indistinguishable, but T. saginata and T. asiatica have more uterine branches (15–30) compared to T. solium (8–12).
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SAGINATA AND TAENIASIS ASIATICA

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.question: Which medication is considered highly effective for treating Taeniasis saginata with a single dose?
items.explanation: A single dose of praziquantel (10 mg/kg) is highly effective for treating Taeniasis saginata.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.question: What temperature must beef be exposed to for at least 5 minutes to destroy cysticercosis?
items.explanation: Exposure to temperatures as low as $56^{	ext{o}}$C for 5 minutes will destroy cysticercosis in beef.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common location for cysticerci in humans?
items.explanation: Cysticerci are commonly found in the central nervous system, skeletal muscle, and subcutaneous tissue, but not typically in the liver.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)
items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of neurocysticercosis?
items.explanation: Seizures are the most frequent clinical manifestation associated with inflammation surrounding cysticercus in neurocysticercosis.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · Taeniasis saginata and Taeniasis asiatica | TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria
items.question: Which imaging finding is considered diagnostic for neurocysticercosis?
options: Cystic lesion with a mural nodule consistent with a scolex ('dot-in-hole')
Multilobulated cystic lesions in the subarachnoid space
items.explanation: A 'dot-in-hole' appearance is diagnostic for neurocysticercosis.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria

items.subtopic: TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria
items.question: Which imaging modality is more sensitive than CT for identifying small cystic lesions and scolexes in neurocysticercosis?
items.explanation: MRI is more sensitive than CT for identifying small cystic lesions and scolexes.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria

items.subtopic: TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria
items.question: Which finding is considered only minor criteria for diagnosing neurocysticercosis?
options: Obstructive hydrocephalus or enhancement of the basilar meninges on CT
Detection of specific antibodies to T. solium
Histologic demonstration of the parasite from biopsy
items.explanation: Obstructive hydrocephalus or enhancement of the basilar meninges are considered minor criteria.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · TAENIASIS SOLIUM AND CYSTICERCOSIS (part 2) | 1. Absolute criteria | a. Major neuroimaging criteria

items.subtopic: b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria
items.question: Which neuroimaging finding is considered confirmatory for cysticercosis?
options: Resolution of cystic lesions spontaneously or after cysticidal drug therapy
Presence of round lesions 5–20 mm in diameter with no midline shift on CT
Detection of specific anticysticercal antibodies by EITB
items.explanation: Resolution of cystic lesions spontaneously or after therapy is a confirmatory neuroimaging criterion.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria

items.subtopic: b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria
items.question: What is the significance of migration of ventricular cysts in neurocysticercosis?
options: It is documented on sequential neuroimaging studies as confirmatory evidence.
It suggests a false-positive result for cysticercal antigens.
It confirms the presence of systemic disease.
items.explanation: Migration of ventricular cysts documented on sequential neuroimaging is a confirmative criterion.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria

items.subtopic: b. Confirmative neuroimaging criteria | a. Major clinical/exposure criteria
items.question: Which diagnostic test for cysticercosis has over 99% specificity and sensitivity in patients with multiple cysts?
items.explanation: EITB using lentil lectin-purified glycoproteins is >99% specific and sensitive for multiple cysts.

items.question: What is the preferred approach for managing hydrocephalus caused by cysticerci in cerebral ventricles?
options: Administration of antiparasitic drugs prior to surgery
items.explanation: The preferred approach is removal of the cysticercus via neurosurgery.

items.question: When should antiparasitic drug treatment be initiated in patients with neurocysticercosis?
options: After stabilization with antiseizure and anti-inflammatory medications
Before any other medication is administered
Only after surgical removal of cysts
items.explanation: Antiparasitic drug treatment should wait until patients are stabilized.

items.question: Which combination of drugs is more effective in patients with more than two cystic lesions?
items.explanation: A combination of albendazole and praziquantel is more effective.

items.question: Which species is primarily responsible for causing cystic echinococcosis in humans?
items.explanation: E. granulosus sensu lato is responsible for cystic echinococcosis in humans.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 1)

items.question: In which regions is alveolar echinococcosis most prevalent?
options: Western China, central Asia, the Mediterranean region
Alpine, sub-Arctic, and Arctic regions of the northern hemisphere
Eastern Africa and parts of South America
items.explanation: Alveolar echinococcosis is prevalent in Alpine, sub-Arctic, and Arctic regions of the northern hemisphere.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What type of cysts does E. multilocularis form?
items.explanation: E. multilocularis forms multilocular, small irregular cysts.

items.question: Which organ is involved in nearly all E. multilocularis infections?
items.explanation: The liver is involved in nearly all E. multilocularis infections.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms may mimic cholelithiasis due to compression or leakage associated with hepatic cystic echinococcosis?
items.explanation: Compression of a bile duct or leakage into the biliary tree may present with symptoms mimicking cholelithiasis.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential acute presentation following rupture of a hydatid cyst?
options: Fever, pruritus, urticaria, eosinophilia, or anaphylaxis
Pleural chest pain and hydatid empyema
items.explanation: Rupture of a hydatid cyst may present acutely with fever, pruritus, urticaria, eosinophilia, or anaphylaxis.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging modality is particularly useful for identifying pulmonary cysts due to E. granulosus sensu lato?
items.explanation: Chest x-ray or CT can identify pulmonary cysts due to E. granulosus sensu lato.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is a diagnostic feature of cystic echinococcosis on imaging studies?
options: Presence of daughter cysts within the larger cyst
items.explanation: Identification of daughter cysts and eggshell or mural calcification are diagnostic features.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · ECHINOCOCCOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following cyst types may respond well to chemotherapy with albendazole alone?
options: CE1, CE2, and CE3b lesions <5 cm in diameter
CL, CE1, and CE3a lesions <5 cm in diameter
CE2 or CE3b lesions >5 cm in diameter
Large CE1 lesions communicating with the biliary tree
items.explanation: CL, CE1, and CE3a lesions less than 5 cm in diameter may respond well to chemotherapy with albendazole alone.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · CYSTIC HYDATID DISEASE

items.question: What is a contraindication for the PAIR procedure?
options: Cysts communicating with the biliary tree
items.explanation: Contraindications to PAIR include cysts communicating with the biliary tree, large cysts (>10 cm), or superficial cysts.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · CYSTIC HYDATID DISEASE

items.question: What is the recommended duration for continuing albendazole after a PAIR procedure?
items.explanation: Albendazole should be continued for at least 4 weeks after the PAIR procedure.
items.reference: Cestode Infections · CYSTIC HYDATID DISEASE

items.question: What is the primary treatment for patients who have undergone presumed curative resection of E. multilocularis infection?
items.explanation: Patients should be treated with albendazole for at least 2 years after presumptively curative surgery.

items.question: Why is indefinite treatment with albendazole required in some cases of E. multilocularis infection?
options: Liver transplantation eliminates the need for further treatment
items.explanation: Immunosuppression required to prevent rejection of a transplanted liver also promotes proliferation of E. multilocularis and reinfection.

options: Nitazoxanide 500 mg twice a day for 3 days

options: By ingesting uncooked cereal and other foods contaminated by fleas or insects
Through direct contact with infected animals

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended method for preventing diphyllobothriasis?
options: Heating fish to 54°C for 5 minutes or freezing at -18°C for 24 hours.
Applying antiparasitic treatment to dogs and cats.
Using poultices with infected flesh from snakes, frogs, or birds.
items.explanation: Heating fish to 54°C for 5 minutes or freezing at -18°C for 24 hours kills the larval forms of Diphyllobothriid tapeworms, preventing diphyllobothriasis.

items.question: Which of the following statements about sparganosis is true?
options: It commonly presents with gastrointestinal symptoms.
Infection can be acquired by ingestion of water containing infected Cyclops.
The main treatment is parenteral vitamin B12.
Dogs and cats are definitive hosts for Spirometra tapeworms.
items.explanation: Sparganosis can be acquired through the ingestion of water containing infected Cyclops, which are intermediate hosts in the life cycle of Spirometra tapeworms.
